title: 2026/04/30 EDSI [Part 1]
displayTitle: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council; Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Autho [Part 1]
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council; Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Autho
description: Thursday 30 April 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council; Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority Submitters Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council Accreditation Council - Nicole Anderson, Chair - Brian Young, CEO Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Authority Broadcasting Standards Authority - Stacey Wood, Chief Executive - Susie Staley, Chair - Samantha Kennedy, Acting Legal Manager
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council; Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting Standards Autho. Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Accreditation Council; Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of the Broadcasting...

chunks.text: I believe we have Nicole Anderson and Brian here. It's fresh. Thank you so much, and we have half an hour with you. So once you're ready, you can start with some opening remarks, and then there will be questions from members. Thank you. Thank you. Lovely. Well, kia ora everyone. Thank you for having us. I guess we're probably ready to start rolling.

Great. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Kia ora kōrua. Welcome to the committee, and thank you for joining us today and making time in person. Sorry, is that a question? Yes. Oh, sorry. Hold on. They don't want to- You don't have any opening remarks, did you say? Oh, would you like us to open with some remarks? We possibly could do that.

She's almost done. I don't have mine. Yeah, if you could do that, that would be great. Thank you. And if members could- No worries... kindly put your questions through the Chair, that would be really helpful. Yeah, I was really happy to hand over straight away for questions, but appreciate you- I was also know-... offering the opportunity. Yeah. Okay. Do you have some remarks, it's a- I think we could probably fairly comfortably go straight to questions.

Okay, that's fine. He doesn't want to hear, but sure. Okay. All right, so we have first question, Irina, would you- Yeah. Thank you. I think the committee's really interested in your work around working with schools. That's something that was highlighted in your annual report, in the work program, to do with testing for asbestos and other contaminants that young people are exposed to in those environments. It's also a really public function.

Something that would be helpful if you could reflect on how government gets value from that as well as public value. Can you tell us about what's been spent on that work, what kind of value you're seeing, and whether there's an opportunity this financial year to continue and grow that? It's a fantastic question, and Brian will certainly be able to give you detail.

Yeah. So what has been spent on that has been time to date. So working with the Ministry of Education to give them an understanding of how accreditation would benefit the asbestos inspection within the schools. There's a curious situation in our regulations where testing of asbestos must be done in accredited labs, but the inspection of asbestos and surveying doesn't require accreditation.

So the asbestos can be pulled out and then run through an accredited lab, and it looks great. But it's not because you need to go through a well-defined process to do the inspection, and not everyone follows those kind of processes properly. And it's kind of a rubbish in, rubbish out type situation.

So what this is now doing is, in the absence of the regulation for the inspection process to be accredited, MOE has kind of created a form of functional regulation, if you like. So people who tender for that work need to be accredited or on a pathway to accreditation, recognizing it takes a little while to get accredited and that could delay things even more. So the value that we provide is ensuring that the organizations that are doing that inspection work are competent.

Mm-hmm. And so because I'm not sure how familiar you are with the accreditation process, but it's cyclical. We visit these organizations on a routine basis. We take technical experts with us, and many of our people are actually technical ex- our assessors are technical experts. And so they're not just ticking off a compliance sheet, they're actually looking at people doing what they do, making sure they're doing it properly.

Making sure they have the right quality management systems, they have the right kind of equipment, they've calibrated, et cetera. So they're looking at organizations as a system and making sure that that system is producing a quality outcome. And whereas in the past, we have accredited people for inspection because they've recognized the value, but that's been a race to the bottom really in terms of price.

Mm-hmm. So the organizations that we had accredited gradually gave up their accreditation to save that overhead so they could compete with other organizations- Yeah, that makes sense... that weren't accredited. Just to follow up on that. Mm-hmm. So, how do we ensure then that the cost for accreditation is being borne in the right part of the system?

If you've got these operators who are working with schools, they're probably small operators, many of them regional. Do you have enough scope to design the accreditation system which would obviously protect consumer safety, so the parents and the kids at the- Yeah... school, but also then the people who are working with the hazardous materials. Are we getting that balance right now in the system, the rails that you've got to run on?

I believe so. So this isn't our only inspection program. We have inspection programs across a wide variety of regulatory activities. And not all of our accreditation is required by regulators either. Some of it is entirely voluntary by the organizations that we work with.

So in terms of the balance of cost, the cost varies according to the size of the organization and the scope of their accreditation. In other words, how many different things they're being accredited for. So generally, an asbestos inspection organization, which is generally fairly small and has a fairly limited scope-It's the accreditation is a much lower cost than it is for a large medical testing laboratory or- Mm-hmm... or something of that nature. So the cost does scale.

Another thing that we have done is we have recently introduced something that we call Te Parua recognition, which is like accreditation light, and we differentiate it very carefully, it has its own website and so forth, from our accreditation activities because they are internationally recognized, and we can't kind of get the two confused.

But what it does is provide an even less expensive model because it requires, while we still have onsite evaluation, it does involve more self-evaluation during the accreditation life cycle, which is generally four years. Can I just a final- Supplement, yeah. Just to, thinking about the costly cleanups in the last financial year for schools with asbestos contaminated sand. Different issue.

Should we have a system- Yeah... where there is accreditation for the cleanup of products like that, or do you envision a way to speed up a process which, same industry that you're working with, but a different aspect of what they are doing in the same environment and stuff?

So all of that is possible and it comes down to, I guess, is there a problem that needs to be solved with the cleanup? First and most, I guess, the starting point is the inspection and identifying what needs to be cleaned up. And then having some kind of oversight of that cleanup process that might be an accredited organization or it might not need to be an accredited organization depending on what the various stakeholders regard as appropriate.

Mm-hmm. We stand poised to accredit, but we recognize that accreditation isn't always necessary, and we would be the first to say that there is another way of doing this if it wasn't necessary. Mm-hmm. A supplementary from Dr. Hemesh Gambhir. Yeah, and actually that feeds probably smack bang into my supplementary question.

How do you measure whether accreditation is actually needed? And I appreciate you've got an accreditation light kind of scheme because we've talked a lot about cost, but what about actually value? Well, if you think health and safety is important and it... Well, sorry, it's not meant to imply anything, but I think we all regard health and safety as important.

So if you want to be sure that organizations are acting competently, one way that you can exercise assurance is through the accreditation process. So we work with WorkSafe over their pressure equipment, cranes and passenger ropeways, the so-called PEPPA regulations, and we provide the assessment of competence.

Otherwise it would be a compliance driven process rather than a risk management and outcome driven process that accreditation is. But in terms of whether accreditation is the right tool for the job, that's really down to the organization that wants to make the decision about how important that assessment of competence is to them.

Exactly. Or if there is a clear reason for doing it. So in other words, for example, there are industry issues that are arising and we're probably familiar with the education issues that were in the media and so forth. They were looking for an answer to stop that from happening.

One of the comments from education was actually the cost of an error by an inspection organization was hundreds of thousands of dollars. Yeah. So for that organization that was then complaining about the thousands of dollars it was going to cost to get them initially accredited.

Mm-hmm. The initial process is more expensive than the ongoing process. Then it was- It would've saved money. Yeah. They would've saved a lot of money. Excuse me, that's very embarrassing. Um- And the water would be another example, water testing of course.

Yeah. Water testing. So all water testing, drinking water testing is the laboratories that assess that are accredited laboratories. Mm-hmm. That's a requirement by Taumata Arowai. But actually water supply inspection does not need to be accredited. It used to be.

But, so we are the ambulance at the bottom of the cliff for water testing. And we would like to also be the safety net at the top of the cliff for that as well. That's another thing. Good. New primary from Dr. Vanessa Bennie. Thank you. And, you were talking about health and safety, which is good.

That's my area of questioning really. So in the 2024 cycle, you didn't meet one of your performance targets around new areas for models of health and safety- Mm-hmm... certification. And, I just wonder what you're doing about that for the 2025/26. And also, we're doing through another committee, the Health and Safety bill is going through. And I just wonder, has there been any effect of the fact that we're making some changes on the process for you?

In terms of the health and safety? Yeah. Because we're doing that, has that in any way held up the new models going through? Because there may be some uncertainty about what's required. No, I haven't seen- Okay... any impact of changes to the act holding up any new activities.

It's really down to the regulatory agencies themselves and their appetite for taking on board accreditation. Mm-hmm. From our perspective, we streamline regulations. We provide a scalable solution to regulatory activities. Yeah. We move cost out of government agencies, and potentially inspection and so forth, out of government agencies and into industry, where we would argue it belongs, and we produce a better outcome. So we keep pushing hard at those messages, but regulators tend to have their own toolboxes, and it can be pretty hard to convince agencies that there's a different

way and a better way of doing it. Mm-hmm. There was the other part of your question, I'm sorry. You started off, it was- There. It was about what you're, I think you're starting to- Oh, our measures Yeah. Yeah. So our measures, one of the things that we've tried, because we're very operational. That's what we do. We deliver accreditation services by IANZ, we deliver certification services by TALRSC, and we deliver training services focused on upskilling the organizations that we work with.

Mm-hmm. So it's very operational, and so the easiest way to measure what we do is in just outputs. Yeah. How many people we've trained, how many accreditations, how many certifications, and so forth. But that's not really the message, and it doesn't really convey our values.

So we try and make our performance measures more outcome focused. Mm-hmm. But that's actually quite difficult to do. You can spend a lot on economists doing economic- Yeah... impact studies and surveys, doing client surveys, which we do. We do both of those things. But in the end, we have to try and link the numbers of things that we do to outcomes, and that's where it gets tricky.

So if we say that, "Well, we're going to increase health and safety in New Zealand by having all these new programs," that's great, and we can work really hard to do that. But it requires other organizations to pick up what we're doing and recognize the value and say, "Yes, we want you to do that, and we want you to set up this new program for us." So we are doing that.

For example, a current example is with DOC. Mm-hmm. They had an issue with concessionaires that work on what they call concessionaires, organizations that work on their estate. And so they were concerned that the oversight, the organizations that were essentially certificating them or assessing them may or may not be competent. So we've set up a program for them to assess the competence of these organizations because DOC is a PCBU- Mm-hmm in relation to these concessionaires, so they have a shared responsibility for health and safety with the concessionaires.

So we give them the assurance that they are doing all that's reasonably practicable to ensure that the concessionaires are acting in a way that is consistent with health and safety requirements. Can I just hop on? Supplementary, yep. Yeah. What do you need to do, do you think, to- Mm-hmm ...

increase awareness around the importance of the work you do? Or is it that you rely on the responses of other organizations rather than you needing to be able to explain or, in some ways, advertise what you do? So we do. We're increasing the amount of marketing we're doing.

Mm-hmm. The clearest message from our clients and our client surveys is they want us to communicate the value of accreditation to their clients and their would-be clients, and which we support entirely. But the same issue is there that is for communicating with regulators and others who might want more of what we do because there's a cost to doing that.

Mm-hmm. And we're very operational, operate on cost recovery, and so we really have to manage those cost pressures and- Yeah... our funds entirely come from the revenue from our clients. So the more of that other work that we do, that's still important- Mm... the more we have to charge those clients. So it's quite difficult.

So we're all multi-hatted. And my team and I all do things that are well off our job descriptions because we just recognize someone has to do them, and we can't keep hiring people to do these things. And you've definitely increased your engagement just with- Yeah... different ministries and- Yeah... agencies- Yeah... at a high level.

Just building those- Yeah... relationships, so- So I-... definitely helping. Yeah. I plod the pavements of Wellington quite regularly. And I fight hard to get meetings with people. I have an EA who's very good at sweet-talking other EAs to get me meetings- ... that I can't get by approaching the people directly, so- Yeah. Because there are a lot of people with that same setup. Yeah.

So it's- Ministerial staff... yeah, it's hard work, but it's a process and of course, people are moving in and out of seats too. So- Yeah people I think I'm starting to make progress with- Mm... and then they've moved on, and then it's time to start again.

Mm-hmm. Good work. Thank you. A quick supplementary on that one. So with your fee, how do you decide when you are going to increase it, and how does it work? In the year that we are reviewing, are there any review to that? That's a really good question. This is a value proposition piece that we've been working on for a wee while as a board, and there's going to be a little bit more work to do on it, I think.

But in terms of fee increases, it's around demonstrating that value, isn't it? Because we can't just keep increasing fees- Yes. Forever and ever, I mean. So. So we're doing a lot to improve our efficiencies, and a big piece of work for us is our digital transformation work. We've just down selected to two vendors now, and we had a very exciting session.

I had to leave to go to the airport at the end of it. I'm as focused on this as my whole team is focused on this. Most of the organization is focused on this because our IT systems are out of date and an embarrassment, actually, when we're speaking with our clients. Mm. And so the efficiencies that that will create are significant. We also look at every possible way we can create efficiencies, and a lot of that is by having people multi-hatted.

But it's also trying to automate and to improve scheduling. We have done a huge amount of work over the last five years or so with our people, developing leadership, particularly at the team leader level, but also at senior level and introducing performance development plans, succession planning, talent mapping and so forth to make sure that we're not only trying to be as good as we can be, but positioning ourselves in the future to be as good as we... to maintain that level of competence.

All right. New question from Arina Williams. I wanted to ask you about those opportunities that you see, and we're sort of in that part of the discussion now in not only growing the people that you're working with and serving, but also the revenue streams that you have through the training and the certification.

What are the opportunities that you see? What are the industries that around the world are emerging as industries that need this sort of accreditation? And then what are the opportunities that you see with technological advancement? Yeah. So there are a lot of opportunities that exist now that just aren't being capitalized on. So, agencies that have their own inspectorates that aren't statutory inspectorates, that could be either carved off and become- What's an example of that?

So, for example, vehicle inspection, the heavy vehicle, particularly heavy vehicle certification at Waka Kotahi. There's still different parts of WorkSafe. We're working with hazardous substances at the moment, trying to get them on board, but

it just kind of gets stuck in the middle. There is opportunities for, and not necessarily just to kind of removing or kind of eliminating inspection capabilities within organizations and especially those that have statutory inspection, but actually using accreditation. So one example would be mine inspection. We're all very familiar with the Pike River tragedy, and my understanding is that was because there just aren't enough mine inspectors to go around.

Mm. If you had an accredited inspection that wouldn't be doing the statutory inspection role, it would allow that it would define the risk scape and would be able to point statutory mine inspectors right at the mines that they need to be most concerned about. And then there's no cost apart from the industry for that because there's the cost of the inspection, the extra inspection to the mines, and then there would be the cost of the accreditation to the inspection organizations.

Mm. There's lots of different opportunities across a wide variety of regulators. Just a quick supplementary on the how, what is the proposition and interested in what Dr Vanessa Weinig was asking. How do you decide between those professional bodies and occupational licensing- Mm... and the kind of accreditation that you offer?

Because obviously you're working very closely with industry in this regard. And many of the industries that you regulate have the strong professional bodies which would see this as an occupational licensing issue, which they- Yeah... it's their domain. But then there's the accreditation pathway which you operate. How do you find that balance? So there's two different layers there, and one of the things that we look at in assessing the competence of an organization are, are the people who are doing the work appropriately licensed?

Is their practicing certificate up to date and so forth? These are the kinds of checks. So an area of our work is medical imaging services, and we accredit pretty much all medical imaging services in New Zealand, especially those that do the radiology and CT, MRI scanning in public and private. And the way that started was one private sector organization got on board and thought, "This is a good idea,"

and with point of difference, and we can also be assured, our board of directors can be assured that we're doing everything right. And then others followed because they thought, "Oh, okay, we don't want to get them to have that competitive advantage," or, "We see the value as well." But we don't get into the issue of determining whether a radiologist is competent. That's their college's domain. So we do check that everything is up to date, but we don't try and kind of-Duplicate what has already been done.

Mm-hmm. Just a different- Different, yeah. New one. I want to ask you about the international trend of synthesizing different standards around the world. The New Zealand government has, say in the building sector, been interested in synthesizing our standards with Australia. Are you interested in that? What is your role in a system which is meant to be helping productivity by finding similarities of standards and then adopting those, rather than going through a long process to develop the same, but New Zealand standard?

Yeah. So we work actively in the area of standards development. Yeah. We send people overseas at our own cost to work on ISO standards, so particularly accreditation standards, but other ISO standards too as required. And because it's not just important for us as an organization, it's important New Zealand has a voice in these standards that we will then use, and to make sure that they are fit for us as well as fit for other countries.

So we do that on behalf of Standards New Zealand, so we are essentially the technical experts that do that. We approached Housing and Urban Development and Standards New Zealand about changing the methamphetamine testing standard because it was misaligned with what the current thoughts were around the correct amounts of asbestos that were acceptable within a dwelling.

So we're embedded in that and we see real value in being able to do that, and to make sure that the standards work for us as an organization, but to make sure that they are effective as well for us as a country. Just new primary from Dr. David Wilson.

Yeah. This is sort of following on a little bit from what Hamish was asking, drilling down into the value for money part of the process, because it's on both sides really, isn't it? It's whether your clients believe they're getting value for money from the accreditation- Yeah... or certification, and it's whether or not you believe you're actually providing a service which has value for money, so what you're charging for it, et cetera. And in some ways you're a monopoly.

So you've got a monopolistic, through legislation- Mm-hmm... position. So interested because most of the movement at the moment is towards outcome assessment and performance, and value for money is one of those. So interested in the detail of what you did with your surveys and how you actually are moving towards establishing that they are getting good value for the service and so is the government.

And I have a supplementary to that as well. So in terms of our value for money, so we do do the client survey and we get a lot of good feedback out of that. It's quite interesting because you'll see for something like quite a heavily regulated area like building consent authorities- Yeah you'll see a comment that says, we ask, "Why did you become accredited?" And one comment might say, "Because it makes us..." These are actually quotes.

"Because it makes us a better business." Yeah. And then another might say, "Because the regulator made me." And so different perspectives on the same thing. But in terms of our value for money, so our purpose is very much focused on protecting the health and prosperity of New Zealand through what we do.

And so we work really hard to try and assess that. So the client survey we get scored, I think, pretty highly- That's good... I would say. And we have all kinds of ongoing checks on satisfaction and we're constantly trying to get as much feedback as we can about what we're doing.

I talked about an econometric study. That study suggested if you took IANZ out of the equation, eliminate us right now, it'd be $2.5 billion out of GDP, and our work touches on about $50 billion worth of exports. So we've talked about health and safety, but an important part of what we do is this international side, and we are evaluated in the same way that we evaluate other organizations.

We're evaluated against ISO 17011. So in March next year, we will have an international team coming through and just looking into every nook and cranny and literally watching over shoulders as our people are assessing other organizations and so forth. Sorry, I'm wildly digressing now.

So our value for money proposition is something that we're always trying to be on top of because it's important for us to be able to communicate that message, and it's also really important for our people to understand. And I think they all do. They're all on board with the purpose.

And it's what gets them to work each day. Just want to, as my secondary, just follow up a little bit. I'm glad you mentioned construction and housing and- Yeah... and aligned a little bit to where Arina was going in terms of productivity. I've had said to me on numerous occasions, not just from that industry sector, that if you want to improve productivity in New Zealand, you get rid of the silliness in health and safety. So the alignment with international standards I think is really, really important here because are we perceived

internationally as too safety-ist? Mm-hmm. And what does that mean? That's a very big question. I couldn't answer how we're perceived internationally in that respect. I know-That if-... accreditation was used more widely, it would vastly impact health and safety in New Zealand because it's almost infinitely scalable, or scalable to the extent that the populace and the industry and so forth would allow it.

But you can, as long as there are people available to inspect, then they can be accredited. And so rather than just saying, "You've got to do what's reasonably practicable, and with the benefit of hindsight we discover it wasn't reasonably practicable, we're going to prosecute the heck out of you,"

you can start being actually more proactively monitoring critical areas of health and safety to ensure that it's appropriate. But in terms of how we compare internationally, I go to international meetings with my accreditation peers, and that's not really something that is ... I know that there are areas, for example, that we don't accredit that are accredited overseas, but that's- A tough question.

Yeah, it is. Rick, one of my jobs is also to keep an eye on time. So we have run out of time that we had with you, so thank you so much for answering. Thank you. And- Yeah... I can see that members have a lot of interest in the work that you do, so there might be some written questions afterwards. Yes. I'm happy to answer those. Thank you so much.

Thank you very much for your time- Thank you all very much... and it's- Have a great day... we really appreciate the opportunity to speak with you about the work that we do. Yeah. Thank you. Well done. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
Thank

you so much for your time. I have two names here, so I have Stacey Wood here. Yeah, thank you so much. And Suzy Stanley? Suzy is joining us online today. Oh, great. But with me, I have Samantha Kennedy. Samantha Kennedy. Thank you so much, and yeah, welcome to our select committee. And we have one hour with you, so if you would like to start with some opening remarks, and then we will have questions from members.

Thank you. So do we need to wait for Suzy? She can't see her online yet. She does have the Zoom link. Okay. So- But we- We are early are we? Can begin a few- Yeah, we can? Okay, great. Thank you. Great. Yeah. Kia ora. It's good to be here again. We were last here in August 2025.

So I won't refresh you too much on who we are- Yeah... but I will just make some comments about the time that we've had since then, which has been somewhat eventful. The BSA is, as you know, an independent Crown entity, a quasi-judicial tribunal, governed by a four-person board which is supported by eight staff, including myself.

We exist to uphold standards in broadcasting, as the name suggests. Our act, the Broadcasting Act 1989, defines our jurisdiction, and that definition is programs. It's audio visual content transmitted to the public by any means of telecommunication, whatever that means. Mm-hmm. As you know, this is an issue that's been the topic of a lot of commentary in the last six months. We do need legislative clarity to bring our act into the present or replace it entirely.

Even our most traditional broadcasters are moving towards digital-only offerings, and they need clarity as well. So we agree that the current model needs to change, but we do consider there's still an important role for a different and modern media regulator. In the meantime, we must apply our act as it is, and our vision in doing that is freedom of expression without harm.

Simple to say, but not always simple to give effect to. We've got three main areas of focus for achieving that vision, and the first is the most public-facing, which is complaints determination, providing the public with a free and independent complaints service about whether a broadcast content has breached standards or not.

The second is oversight and development of those standards, which we do in a co-regulatory way in consultation with broadcasters. We commission independent research that informs how the standards are maintained and applied and gives insight into what the public expect of us and of broadcasters. The third is engagement and education, so that's helping people to understand the standards, promoting media literacy, and how to make informed media choices, but also ensuring that the authority understands community values and public expectations.Our

workload has fluctuated over the last few years, as it's always done. So in the early years of COVID, we experienced near-record complaint numbers, but these have fallen significantly in the last couple of years, and we received only 90 complaints last year. Mm. It's too soon to say if this is a pendulum swing or a trajectory that's going to continue. We suspect the latter.

And this is partly a marker of changes in how people consume media. But it's also a marker of success. We find that when we invest more in our non-complaints functions, unmerited complaints go down, and breaches are now relatively rare because broadcasters understand the standards so well. So our uphold rates are low, typically under 10%, where they were once closer to 30% or 40%.

Levies continue to decline, and we expect them to keep doing so, given the fragility and the fragmentation of the media sector. Levies are only paid by broadcasters with revenue over 500K each year, and that's based on a formula set out in the Act. So there's relatively few entities over that threshold and many more smaller broadcasters who don't pay levies.

In the last three years, we've received our first increases to crown funding in 20 years, which has allowed us to weather those falling levies and the effects of inflation. And our expenditure is carefully managed, but it varies often due to factors outside of our control, such as the number of appeals to the High Court and complaints workload.

After a period of over two years in which none of our decisions were appealed, we did have four appeals in the relevant financial year. One was the subject of a High Court decision last year upholding the authority's decision in dismissing the appeal on all grounds, and we're still waiting on decisions on the other appeals. All of our appeals in recent years have been from complainants unhappy that we have not upheld their complaint, not from broadcasters.

So while we wait for the outcome of the current media reform proposals, we continue to make changes where we plan to operate efficiently in a changing environment. We last reviewed our code in 2022, which we do periodically to make sure it's still relevant. Unless there's legislative reform, we will soon need to review it again and see whether it can be improved and adapted within the confines of the current Act.

Finally, we know that broadcasters still deal with many more complaints than are ever referred to us, and they treat complainants with respect and care, which leads to relatively low referral numbers. So I just want to acknowledge the resource and the effort that goes into that. We do think that ours has been a model of a successful co-regulatory system, and that there's a lot that can be carried forward to a more modern model.

So thank you for the chance to be here again. I welcome any questions. Thanks, Jacqueline. And carrying on, we have a question from Reuben Davidson. Thank you, Chair, and thank you for those opening remarks and comments. Really good scene set. I think that two references that you alluded to, one is the Broadcasting Standards Authority and the other is 1989, the Broadcasting Act.

So two terms that are not really fit for 2026 by lots of arguments. And in fact, the chair, Suzy, states in legislation that it's obsolete and that the sphere of influence is shrinking. So I guess my first question really is why or does the BSA remain a fit-for-purpose regulator, and what specific harms is it preventing that no other body can?

The model is not fit for purpose. It does need to change. We've acknowledged it and have acknowledged it for about 15 years now. So, a complaint-driven model where complaints come in and a decision comes out several months later about specific pieces of content is not suitable for how people consume media now.

It needs to be different. It's not our job to decide what the best model is. We have expertise and experience, but what we can say is that the current model is out of date. Mm-hmm. However, there is still value in what we do. The kind of harms that we have a role in preventing are still hugely relevant to society now.

When we look at the likes of the recent social cohesion report that's just come out, which identifies consumption of a broad range of media, the harms of social media, the role that media plays in a healthy democracy, and the effects of inaccurate news.

Having an independent regulator gives people somewhere to turn to get information about where they can get reliable, accurate information about the world around them. It has a role to play in the research that we do that tells us the effect that media's having, what people expect and protecting communities who are more vulnerable and won't necessarily be protected by taking someone to court for defamation but may be protected better by having an accessible, free service that they can go to when they see something in broadcasting that harms them.
So do you want to- Suck on that. So

Is what you're saying, part of what you're saying, that whilst you don't have any ability over regulating online content, you do have a substantial body of work from over many previous years that charts the transition across and the harms or risks of that, so essentially sort of an evidence body to guide further regulation?

That's exactly right. The mediums may have changed, but the message has not. News media has always accounted for the majority of our complaints as opposed to entertainment media, and continues to do so, and news media will always have an important role however it's being delivered.

So all right. You have a supplementary? Yeah. Okay. Just following on from that. Yeah. There are five reform proposals currently, five media reform proposals. There's been no decisions or progress, or no formal decisions taken on the regulatory proposal. How many times has the minister met with the BSA board or chief executive around those proposals?

Good question. I think we have had three or four meetings since the reform proposals came out with the minister. We meet more regularly with the ministry policy team. And further sup on that. Yeah. You've received a letter of expectation from the minister?

We have, yes. Yeah. Are there any specific expectations in your work program delivered in that letter around that reform or around potential reform? Just to continue to work closely with the policy team as we have done and provide our expertise and input where we can.

I don't think that's verbatim wording. Don't quote me, but that's the expectation. And has there been any indication of the future role of the BSA or the future shape? No. So yeah, I have a question here. So in your opening remarks here, you said that the model is and about the complaints, if we look at the trend, are they telling us something?

Is it because that BSA doesn't have that kind of role it used to have before because lot of media consumption is shifting online? Sorry, in terms of falling complaint numbers? Yeah. We have had- Because people think that it's not in your jurisdiction, so the complaints are not coming to you.

Yeah. Certainly, and the recent jurisdiction decision, I think, illustrated that quite well and that it was our first, and I think people were surprised to hear that it was the first time we'd received a formal complaint about an online broadcaster. Certainly, it's not something that people broadly thought was within our remit.

Mm-hmm. But in terms of falling complaint numbers, they have gone up and down a lot over the years. So there's been quite a bit of fluctuation and when you look at political events over that time, you can sort of link it to times of increased public anxiety or major world events going on.

And so there's been peaks and troughs over the years, but they have been, in the last two years, lower and we do think that that is at least partly tied to decreasing viewers of linear television and listeners of radio. But also, there's a couple of other factors of our complaint system not being a good tool for achieving results.

Mm-hmm. Despite what people think, we can't actually shut broadcasters down. We can't control what they say. We issue a decision several months later, and even if it's upheld, a message has gone around the world 10 times by then. So I think fewer people complain to us, because it's not going to get the result that they want.

There's also, from some people, we hear an unwillingness to complain because of we're a public body. Those decisions get published. Unless there's a good reason for name suppression, a complainant's name goes out there, and even if it's suppressed, sometimes it goes out there. And people cop flak for that sometimes.

So if something's going to go online with your name on it, perhaps you don't want to make a complaint to a public body. Mm-hmm. And a further question. This is BSA versus the platform. So just akin to understand that, how do you see that platform is under your remit?

Is under our remit? By applying the wording of the act, which explicitly refers to programs transmitted by radio waves or other means of telecommunication. So there's been quite a lot of analysis done on that, which I won't take the time to repeat. But the authority's legal reasoning is contained within its decision, and it's also informed by external legal analysis that we received.

So then in that case, what do you say about the Facebook commentators, those who do live streaming? Yeah. If it was easy, the act would be fine. Wouldn't need updating. Mm-hmm. But we have applied a pragmatic and purposive interpretation to the definition in the act.

It needs to be transmitted to the public. So, everyone tuning in at the same time is to be receiving the same content, which differentiates it from videos that you can choose and access at any time. The act is silent on territoriality, which is difficult. If we had the resources, maybe we'd People would think we could take over the internet, but that's clearly not the intention or the spirit or the purpose of the act.

So applied that lens in interpreting the definition in a plain English and purposive way. Okay. So then in that case, I'm interested to understand, how does BSA apply to Parliament TV then? Parliament TV files a liberty return. Sorry? Parliament TV files a liberty return each year and has always been subject to our jurisdiction.

It's been always subject to. All right. And then how can people make complaints about it? What is the process to make a complaint about Parliament TV then? Yeah. So our process is that for most complaints, unless it's a privacy breach, complaints must go to the broadcaster first. People can contact the authority if they're not sure how to contact a broadcaster, and we'll do our best to assist. And then, complaints are referred to us if a complainant's unhappy with the broadcaster's response or doesn't receive a response.

So that means the complaint should go to who is running the Parliament TV or to Parliament, and then sent to you BSA. Yeah. I can get you the contact details of the broadcaster. Yes. And do you receive any complaints for Parliament TV? No. You've never received any complaints? I don't believe so. Okay. Thank you. And we go back to Ruben Devon.

Just some stats on the area that you've been exploring, Madam Chair. Do you have any sense of the cost implications of the current platform or BSA investigation or complaint process for the platform? Yes. It's obviously taken up a lot of staff time internally.

Not just the complexity of the complaint itself, but the ensuing communications that we've received, OIA requests, abusive emails, et cetera. It all takes time to deal with. In terms of external cost, I think there was no expenditure obviously within the relevant financial review period because we didn't receive the complaint until after that.

But it's also do we include expenditure that we've had over the years on the act and the application to new media entities as they emerge, because this isn't the first time that we've had to consider this. Mm-hmm. But we have spent less than $50,000 in the last 10 years on external legal advice on jurisdiction issues.

So that gives you a bit of a flavor. We do a lot with a little. Yeah. And just to probe a little more around that distinction between online content that can be called broadcast and online content that can't. So am I right in your previous answers, were you suggesting that a live stream would be seen as a broadcast sitting on a digital platform, but pre-produced content sitting on a digital platform available for consumption when a viewer chooses wouldn't be seen as a broadcasting standards authority consideration?

And also, I think you mentioned about the continuity. You mentioned about the continuity as well in your response. Continuity? Yeah. And just adding that to Ruben's question. Yeah. So there isn't a one to two sentence definition I can give you that will answer every case scenario. But broadly, yes.

So- If people are choosing episodes from a menu, probably out. Yeah. If people are tuning in and listening or watching whatever's on, probably in. But clearly not intended to capture individual random TikToks or Facebook, people's personal things that are not meant to be for- So then a couple more questions about that because with news and current affairs content that is broadcast in New Zealand, some of that is live, some of it's pre-recorded.

So it's less about live and pre-recorded versus is it being streamed. Because most broadcast television isn't live, right? It's all been pre-produced before. Yeah. You would know. But it's about are you clicking a button to tune into a station or a channel or a website and hearing or seeing what everyone else is seeing at the same time.

So then the next part of that would be that some news and current affairs content in New Zealand, Q&A would be a really good example of this, will run a pre-produced broadcast program with an interview, and then they'll host the full interview- Mm-hmm... as a selectable piece of content on their platform.

Yeah. No pun intended with on their digital platform. By the definition that you're suggesting, that would be excluded from the jurisdiction of the BSA, but the post-produced version of that content wouldn't be. Yeah. Look, there has always been a lot, well, at least since the internet began being capable of hosting media, there's been inconsistencies. So for example, under the preexisting scenario, we could take a complaint about a promo ad for a program that, so the promo runs on TV 2, but it's for a program that's

showing on TVNZ Plus only. So we could take a complaint about the promo, but not the actual show because it's on demand. The Media Council and ourselves have areas of overlap and fragmentation, so there's cases where we'll get a complaint about a RNZ spoken word article on the radio, and they'll get a complaint about the written writeup, and sometimes there's different decision outcomes because the headline on the written article substantially changes the meaning of the message. So there have been gaps in fragmentation since our act

began, but they're only getting worse. Mm-hmm. 39 minutes. Just a new supplementary. You mentioned the trending complaints that you've been seeing, and the unwillingness to complain. I think you're describing a situation where most New Zealanders, a vast majority, want a civil public discourse, and yet they are not complaining to you because they're getting used to this outrage that they're seeing constantly.

What are you learning from the complaints that you've received this year as opposed to perhaps 10 years ago, when there were more people emailing you about their views about something that they had heard was offensive, and how do we renegotiate that at present? How are you seeing the trends and the information that you're receiving from members of the public, and turning that into guidance that is useful to us to navigate that?

Yeah. Some really deep questions there. And I think thinking about how we promote greater social cohesion and get away from the toxic discourse online is not something we're fully equipped to deal with. But we know that our broadcasters are dealing with even more abusive and harassing and just unacceptable communication than we are, and that they've had to put safety measures in place to protect their staff.

That's what I said. We see it in complaints, in the number of complaints that are not, "This broadcast was factually wrong, but I didn't agree with it, so it must be biased." Or people not wanting to have viewpoints out there that they disagree with.

So I think that that is influenced by the algorithmic nature of what a lot of people are consuming. But that's speculation. We haven't done deep research on that element. We did do some research on public trust in media, which we published last year, which broadcasters have told us they found useful and supports similar and adjacent research that they've done themselves. And it did reinforce that people are often aware of this notion that they are bringing their own biases to how they consume media, but that doesn't stop them having them .

But they still want accuracy, balance, and fairness in the news that they consume, and they see that as important and drivers of trust. Could I just- Can I sup on that? Yeah. Hold on. No, you carry on with your sup. Oh, thank you. You describe a situation where most broadcasters in New Zealand know what the standards are. But I think most New Zealanders would think that they're seeing more outrageous, more inflammatory content in their traditional media and their social media.

Mm-hmm. Why is this growing divide between what most New Zealanders would expect as sort of a more civil discourse and their ability to raise that with someone, with an ability to put a complaint through to something that would then be able to deal with that?

Yeah, look, broadly, our traditional broadcasters do apply the standards well, and even though there's often quite a lot of commentary around how the news is told, I think it's easier for that noise to be louder now. Our statistics don't indicate that that is a demonstration of falling standards.

Mm-hmm. We do get complaints about the news. We have upheld less than 10% of complaints in the last few years. Broadcasters are generally doing a good job, but there's a lot of criticism out there. Mm-hmm. From all ends of the spectrum. It's not limited. We get as many complaints about traditional media being biased to the left as we do to the right.

When you compare our organizations to what Australia is doing, in living social media platforms, more of a move to providing a useful service to media, which also need this, because you describe a situation where they receive the complaints, they receive the abuse. If you had something else that was the independent arbiter, perhaps it would be really valuable for them.

How do you internationally benchmark the kinds of complaints that you're receiving and the kinds of decisions that you can make? It's tricky because we don't have a directly comparable system overseas, and it is so nuanced that we follow what's happening in Australia, we follow what's happening in the UK with Ofcom, and have connections at those regulators that we sort of try and look for commonalities.

But the regulatory systems are different. Australia has a similar issue to us in their legislation, which they've put a band-aid on with a temporary legislative mechanism to exclude internet broadcasts, but which will require a more permanent solution at some point.

The UK's obviously got a massively well-resourced regulator that's attempting to do a lot of new things in online spaces, but facing big challenges in doing that as well. Mm-hmm. A supplementary question. Yeah. Thank you. And you touched on it briefly, public confidence in our mainstream media is probably at an all-time low.

What role does the BSA play in actually increasing that trust and confidence for the general public? Probably a lot of these broadcasters probably push things right to the limit. What role does the BSA play in actually increasing public confidence? So the most recent research indicates we might have-Come past the lowest point and be on an uptick, but it's too early to tell if that's a long-term trend.

We support trust in media by upholding the standards, publishing our decisions, which tell people when the standards haven't been met. Our research, both our internally-facing research, which tests our decisions with the public to see if they agree with where we've got to on different issues, and the externally-facing research, like our public trust in media research, which helps broadcasters to know that they are doing everything they can to move the dial on that.

But while there are things that media can do and should do to maintain trust, there are also factors that are undermining trust in media that are not the media's responsibility. Online discourse is not always helpful. So it's a complex problem with no silver bullet. But our role, as an independent regulator, is to identify when the standards haven't been met, support broadcasters to uphold those standards, and almost provide a credibility badge. We are media that is subject to a regulator, so if we get it wrong, you can do something about it.

Supplementary, Brendan. Yeah, thank you. Just to seek out a little more information around that idea of where complaints come from. And sometimes, given you've talked about quite extensive monitoring and research that you've done in and around how this all shifts and moves.

So the question here really is around the online amplification of offense or dissatisfaction with broadcast media content being the catalyst for formal complaints that do fit within the current, albeit not fit for purpose, potentially, remit of the BSA.

So someone, for example, posting on Facebook about something they don't like that they saw on the news, and then that pushes a number of complaints to the broadcaster and potentially, more formally, to you. Do you monitor that? And if so, how do you monitor that? Short answer, no. We don't have the resources to monitor social media, so we don't investigate where our complaints are coming from. We take them at face value.

Broadcasters may be doing more of that themselves, but I doubt that they have the resources either. So if you were to see an influx of complaints about a specific incident or content- Yeah... do you have any mechanism to reverse look at that? Like if you got 100 complaints about one thing, it'd be very unusual.

There's no trigger point at which point you would work backwards to find out where that's coming from? It's not something that's been an issue for us. And if we have had a number of emails about a specific issue, it's usually pretty clear where that's come from. It'll either be a media article about us, which we do monitor media mentions of us, or it will be a prominent critic.

So then, the awareness is only anecdotal then? It's not recorded or monitored formally. We do monitor media discussions, but we don't go looking for every Facebook post about the broadcasting standard authority. Yeah. Okay. I think Suzy wants to say something. I see your hand up, Suzy.

Thank you very much. And I'm sorry I can't be there, but it was the change of date. Look, I think it's an interesting question. We do see trends and, as you'll all be aware, the controversial issues around Gaza, Palestine, Israel, and sometimes we do see similar complaints as Stacey has said, and they could look like they've been generated from a group.

So we certainly keep a record of that. But there's no consistent sort of complaint mechanism. We also occasionally have seen, and of course, we also are responsible in an election year, fair to say, Stacey, that we have seen occasionally a group who have got together and wanted to petition, if you like, one particular point of view, and they stand out because we get so few.

So we certainly keep a record of those. But as Stacey says, it's not an online search that we do or monitor. We simply don't have the resources. But we can see trends. As I've said to the select committee previously, clearly during the COVID issues, and I'm sure you all who were there experienced them, there were quite a number of small groups who made very similar complaints and repeatedly.

So that's probably as far as we can go. So I hope that helps. Yeah, it does. Just a final clarification question on that. Is there a resource or cost implication to that for the BSA? To? To campaigns, not to overstate it, but campaigns of directing people towards engaging in a formal complaint process about specific content Well, if I could answer that. Well, the cost will be that it's just a lot more work. On the other hand, we would triage them anyway, and we would look at, essentially, if someone's complaining about the same

matter, you would take them under that one complaint. So, on the other hand, you'd just say, "We're doing our job. We've got complaints, so we're dealing with them." But you can sift through them. Our team are very good at triaging them and saying, "Well, these are really the same complaint from five different people." And so, it would be fair to say, Stacey, we would deal with it that way.

Correct. Yeah. It hasn't really been a huge issue in our formal complaints space, though. Because they have to go through broadcasters first, and that usually whittles down the numbers. And we only had 90 formal complaints last year, so fairly not getting hundreds on- Yeah... one issue. Okay. Thank you. So that kind of is a supplementary, but also is related to questions I asked before about Parliament TV.

So do you then expect that Parliament TV should be stating their usual statement that usually broadcasters do about the complaint process? Because I haven't seen Parliament TV doing that. Have you seen Parliament TV doing that, and how do you I haven't seen Parliament. I don't watch Parliament TV very often.

Oh, I just- I'm not aware if they're- Better than Shortland Street... doing the broadcaster publicity notices. We- Hear... made a decision a few years ago not to actively monitor broadcaster publicity notices because we don't have a mechanism to enforce compliance and we had been facing increased resource constraints in the last few years, so we've operated on a honesty system.

But then how does the public know that this process exists? Through publicity notices- Yes. Yes... through our website, through broadcasters having an obligation to tell complainants that they have a right to refer complaints to us. So the act requires broadcasters to have a public-facing complaints process and a method for dealing with those, and to tell complainants that they have the opportunity to refer to us if they don't So in a way, it's then not a requirement for the broadcaster to broadcast that statement about how to make a complaint.

It is a requirement to do that, but there's nothing in the act that gives us any enforcement powers over that. So then you say there's a requirement, but there is no enforcement. It kind of is conflicting. So the act says broadcasters must do this.

But the act doesn't say if they don't, the authority can X, Y, and Z. So in what happened recently with BSA versus the platform, do you expect platform to broadcast that kind of statement about how to make a complaint? I don't think that's a realistic expectation.

Okay. Georgie Denzie. Thank you. Kia ora. Thank you. This has been really informative. I'm interested in performance, and I can see that performance on all levels is doing really well, financial performance, et cetera. But I'm just thinking about the future and if there are changes in this space, what does that look like for the future of financial performance and your performance indicators across operations?

If there are changes in the legislation- Well, I guess I'm saying if things don't change- Mm-hmm... if the legislation stays just as it is- Mm-hmm... how will that impact your performance? Look, it's really difficult to predict. Some of it's easier to predict than other aspects.

We can probably safely predict that levies won't increase and are likely to decrease further. Those levies are set in the act, so we can't change how much we charge or who we charge. But in terms of complaints numbers, we can probably predict they won't get hugely higher. They may get somewhat higher than we've seen in the last couple of years because those have been historically low numbers.

We haven't had a huge influx suddenly of online complaints since August, October last year, when it became more publicly known that we can accept those. So remains to be seen how that will affect our workload. Appeals are always an unpredictable aspect of our workload. We went for about two and a half years of having no High Court appeals and then had four come at once. So, that means that we have to then engage legal counsel to assist the High Court in determining those.

Thank you. Okay. There's a supplementary from Ruben Davidson. Yeah. Just on that levy question and the financial performance question. Because online media streamers are not paying any levy, have you had any feedback from qualifying levy payers about the fact that their levies are financing the legal complaints process for non-levy payers?

No. The levy system has always been such that some broadcasters pay more substantial levies than others, and that's not always reflective of the numbers of complaints that they get. It is a very simplistic formula, totally revenue based. You could beearning massive revenue and getting no complaints and paying a big levy to subsidize the system.

Many of our smaller broadcasters have never, and will never, pay a levy. And hypothetically, if you were to add online platforms in and they became levy payers, you would have a sense, I would expect, of the extent to which your levy capture would increase.

We don't have a good idea of that yet, and we would need to work through a process. It's not our assumption that we'd get suddenly a lot more levies due to the revenue threshold that broadcasters need to cross before anything at all is payable. Mm-hmm. So lots more work, but not necessarily any more levy.

Correct. New primary, Huhana-Jones. Yeah. It's a primary, but it sucks off your part, Madam Chair, around community awareness and understanding of the role and function of the Broadcasting Standards Authority, because it's said that you've achieved your community awareness goals for Maori community, but Pacific and Asian we're still underperforming.

How do we even understand that you have that community awareness in community? And then, what are the measures that you're putting in place to achieve those with our Asian and Pacific communities? Yeah. So we do surveys every year or two and target specific communities that we're trying to reach.

And traditionally, it has been more difficult to engage with communities that may not either consume a lot of broadcast media anymore. We know that certain groups, young people, Pasifika, tend to get more media online than through linear. So part of it's just due to audience behavior and expectations, but also it's reaching groups that our research and experience has shown are less likely to complain to an institution like us.

So, if Pacific peoples have a complaint about a broadcaster, they'll often try to resolve that within the community rather than coming to us with it. Mm-hmm. And so that's fine. But we do want to make sure people know that we're here if they do need us. So we do targeted engagement with community groups, with specific broadcasters, like Pacific Media Network, for example, to just make sure that even if people don't want to complain to us, that they know that we exist, that our resources exist, and that they can reach us if they need to.

Mm-hmm. Just to add on to that. I'm wondering about the demographics of your engagement around community awareness. Having sat with a few youth groups recently, how we can target our young people to be more informed of their rights, but then also the role and function of the Broadcasting Standards Authority to support them in voicing concerns and expectations around broadcasters.

Mm. So sorry, what- Yeah. What's your plan for young people? What's our plan for young people? How are you engaging our young people? Yeah. We haven't done any specific research or targeted engagement with rangatahi beyond we do speak with a number of university classes who are studying things like media policy or broadcasting.

But we also know that young people are not big consumers of broadcast content. So it's a bit of a balance of wanting to make sure that we are protecting children's interests, which is one of our standards, but also using our resources to address the communities that are consuming the most broadcast content and are more likely to complain to us. But we have done research in the past on children's interests specifically, and there are tools that we publicize around parental controls and content, so that people can make informed choices.

So even though we haven't done as much engagement as we would if circumstances were different, it is one of the standards, and so broadcasters are required to uphold it. And there's been some decisions on that, where things have been played at an inappropriate time, and that kind of thing.

Just to follow on on that. You've made a comment around how it's those who are most likely to complain. Well, that's not young people usually. No. Yeah. Because they're disengaged and they don't know how to complain about issues. Yeah. And it's more likely that it's an older population that like to lodge complaints- Absolutely... from my experience.

But it's sort of like a rights opportunity- Yeah... to gear up and empower, even if it is social media campaigns or something-... coming from And I know that we're not in the social media space, but I know that the classification office has done a lot of important work in that area around ensuring that people know that they can complain if they see something online.

And that's work that I think is hugely valuable. Mm-hmm. Suzy, you have your hand is up. Yes, I can add to that, Stacy, that we also have a loose partnership with Save the Children in New Zealand. So I'm the chair of Save the Children, and we have an education hub for youth and for primary school because we're aware that teachers are extremely busy, and it's a resource. And I know our communications manager and Save the Children's communications manager have worked together.

So we have some information that's passed through, and as you said, it's a rights issue, and Save the Children is all about children's rights. Kia ora. I should just add that we've also produced a short media literacy resource that we've circulated the schools to use as well.

A sup on this from Reuben Davidson. Yeah, thank you. One of the resources I saw, either in the annual report or on the website, was a series of resources in other languages to make sure that people could follow the complaints process. What I don't recall seeing, and I just may not have seen it, was specific resources for people who are English language speakers but may have accessibility issues.

Mm. Are those are resources you've got as well? So we have done a little bit of work in making our website more accessible by providing Word versions of written resources. However, we're doing a project over this current financial year and the next to improve the accessibility of our website. We did an accessibility audit last year, which showed room for improvement, so we're putting those improvements into place over the next six to 12 months.

Mm-hmm. Just to sup off that briefly. A supplementary, Honore. And just for our- You have a new prime... disabled community, what does that look like in terms of the audit for your website and your engagement with those with disabilities? Yeah. So we've had an expert accessibility audit done for us to show us exactly what needs to be changed to make it easier for people who are using screen readers or who have hearing or vision impairments.

And we will be doing things like making the menus stay dropped down when you drop them down, that kind of technical improvement. Long overdue, but we've sort of been hesitant to do major work on the website knowing that it may not be needed for much longer.

But we have to proceed as though we're going to be around for the foreseeable. Mm-hmm. Dr. Davidson. Yeah, I'll get one shot at this, so excuse me while I frame this. I'm really interested in what you've said today. You started off talking about the fragility and fragmentation in digital media, and I think it relates a little bit to where Huhana was going here, the kind of disengagement in mainstream media and broadcasting. And you said that's not actually high with youth.

Mm-hmm. So that's one issue which kind of goes against the grain in terms of how we're defining broadcasting. And the Broadcasting Standards Authority, the name's on the tin. So defining what broadcasting is, I think, is obviously a real challenge for you. And you mentioned earlier that both the law and the model that you're operating under are not fit for purpose, and you need a more modern model. And you've got a team of eight with a limited budget.

And right in the middle of all of this is huge moral and ethical dilemmas with digital media and broadcasting, if we can call it that. You mentioned also the social cohesion report and the trust in social media. All these things are swirling around. So there's two questions here. One's a look to the future, if I'm allowed that, and the second is, how on earth do you navigate the moral and ethical dilemmas of the complaints that you already have?

So maybe answer that one first and then come back to what the future might look like for us to consider in how we address those things. How do we navigate the complaints that we have? Yeah, through moral and ethical dilemmas that obviously come up. Such as a complaint that a program has breached an ethical standard?

Yeah. Well, we've got 37 years of experience doing that now, and the standards have evolved over time, but there's a pretty solid body of precedent, as well as research we do every year to check that our finger on the pulse is still on the pulse in terms of how the community interprets our standards and what they expect from them. So that part of it is a challenging issue, but we are the expert body at doing that, so we lean on our expertise and experience.

As to the future, if it was easier, it would be done already, right? And countries around the world are grappling with what does sensible media and social media regulation look like. It's not the role of the existing independent regulator to decide what the future should look like.

We can only provide our expertise and advice to those who do have the responsibility for making those decisions. So in some ways, the- Supplementary... the discussion with the complaint with the platform, which is an online digital media platform, does this fit within the broadcasting definition, et cetera, et cetera, is kind of almost like a litmus test for this because that will be testing your expertise, which has firmly been in mainstream media and broadcasting, and now you've opened up a whole new area.

Mm-hmm. So I guess I'm asking how you're going with that in your own team. It's like that's a huge thing to take on. It is and it isn't. There's always been a huge variety of media within broadcasting, and context has always been a factor in every decision. So what's acceptable to an audience of one program will not be acceptable to an audience of another program, and it might be due to the time of the day or the style of program. It's the audience that it's targeting.

So we-If you read some of our decisions on, for example, talk back shows, there'll often be discussion of what the audience will expect from this presenter of this show. And so, in that sense, it's just another show, and it helps that when you click on the button, it does what you expect a TV or a radio to do, and it plays you the show. And seeing and hearing media is not a novel concept.
